fact: A mob of around nine hundred Parisians attacked the Bastille
source: The Fall of the Bastille is widely documented; Simon Schama's 'Citizens: A Chronicle of the French Revolution' (1989) estimates the crowd at roughly 800–1,000 attackers.

fact: The garrison consisted of about thirty Swiss guards and eighty retired soldiers (invalides)
source: The garrison is documented as 82 invalides (veteran soldiers) and 32 Swiss grenadiers from the Salis-Samade regiment. See Jacques Godechot, 'The Taking of the Bastille' (1970).

fact: Ninety-eight attackers were killed during the assault
source: Commonly cited casualty figures are 98 attackers killed and 73 wounded. This figure appears in multiple sources including Godechot's 'The Taking of the Bastille' and is reflected in the Wikipedia summary of the event.

fact: The drawbridge was lowered, and the crowd flooded the courtyard
source: Governor de Launay ordered the inner drawbridge lowered after negotiations/capitulation; the crowd surged in. Described in detail in Simon Schama, 'Citizens: A Chronicle of the French Revolution' (1989), Chapter 10.

fact: The crowd brought stolen cannons to aim at the fortress gate
source: Attackers seized cannons from the Hôtel des Invalides earlier that morning (along with muskets) and brought them to bear on the Bastille. Documented in Godechot, 'The Taking of the Bastille' (1970) and widely corroborated.

fact: Governor de Launay's head ended up on a pike before sunset
source: Governor Bernard-René de Launay was killed by the mob after the surrender; his head was severed and paraded on a pike through Paris. Documented in multiple primary accounts and in Schama, 'Citizens' (1989).
